h1Title: Adrenocortical Insufficiency: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Adrenocortical insufficiency is a condition in which the adrenal cortex produces inadequate amounts of cortisol, and in primary forms also aldosterone, to meet the body's physiological demands. Primary adrenal insufficiency (Addison's disease) affects approximately 1 in 10,000 people in Western countries; secondary and tertiary forms from hypothalamic-pituitary disease and glucocorticoid withdrawal are considerably more common. The hallmark presentation is profound fatigue, weight loss, and hypotension that worsen progressively over months. Without identification and treatment, acute adrenal crisis — circulatory collapse triggered by any physiological stressor — carries a mortality above 20%. Hormone replacement with hydrocortisone and fludrocortisone is life-long but restores near-normal function in most patients.

keyStats.prevalence: Primary adrenal insufficiency: approximately 93-140 per million in Western populations (Arlt & Allolio, Lancet 2003); secondary and tertiary forms much more common, affecting up to 1% of patients on chronic corticosteroid therapy
keyStats.demographics: Primary adrenal insufficiency most common in women aged 30-50; secondary form follows the sex distribution of its pituitary cause; glucocorticoid-induced tertiary form affects any age on long-term corticosteroid therapy
keyStats.avgAge: Primary (autoimmune): onset typically 30-50 years, with a female-to-male ratio of approximately 3:1; secondary: depends on the underlying pituitary pathology
keyStats.globalCases: An estimated 100-200 per million in developed countries have primary adrenal insufficiency; millions more have secondary or tertiary insufficiency from corticosteroid therapy globally

definition: Adrenocortical insufficiency (ICD-10: E27.40) is the clinical syndrome resulting from inadequate secretion of glucocorticoids (primarily cortisol) by the adrenal cortex. Three forms are recognized by anatomical level: primary adrenal insufficiency (Addison's disease) results from destruction or dysfunction of the adrenal cortex itself, causing deficiency of both cortisol and aldosterone with compensatory elevation of ACTH and high plasma renin activity; secondary adrenal insufficiency results from pituitary or hypothalamic disease reducing ACTH secretion, causing cortisol deficiency but preserving aldosterone (which is regulated primarily by renin-angiotensin, not ACTH); tertiary adrenal insufficiency results from long-term exogenous glucocorticoid use suppressing the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis. The distinction between primary, secondary, and tertiary forms determines which hormones require replacement and the expected electrolyte pattern. Glucocorticoids are essential for glucose homeostasis, vascular tone, immune regulation, and the stress response; their deficiency impairs all four. In primary insufficiency, mineralocorticoid deficiency additionally causes sodium wasting, volume depletion, and hyperkalemia. Adrenocortical insufficiency is managed by endocrinologists and requires life-long medical identification and patient education about emergency cortisol dosing during illness or surgery.

typesClassification.description: Destruction of the adrenal cortex by autoimmune disease (70-90% of cases in developed countries), TB (most common cause globally), or other infiltrative processes. Cortisol and aldosterone both deficient; ACTH markedly elevated (above 200 pg/mL). Skin hyperpigmentation from ACTH melanocyte-stimulating hormone activity is the clinical hallmark.

Pituitary or hypothalamic disease (tumors, radiation, surgery, infiltration, Sheehan's syndrome) reduces ACTH secretion. Cortisol deficient; aldosterone normal. No skin hyperpigmentation; electrolytes usually normal; other pituitary hormone deficiencies may coexist.

The most common form overall — caused by suppression of the HPA axis by exogenous glucocorticoids used for any indication. Risk increases with doses of prednisone equivalent above 5 mg/day for more than 4 weeks. Aldosterone preserved; cortisol deficient only during stress or with abrupt withdrawal.

Acute decompensation triggered by physiological stress (infection, trauma, surgery, gastrointestinal illness) in a patient with underlying insufficiency. Presents with severe hypotension, hypoglycemia, and abdominal pain. Medical emergency with mortality above 20% if untreated.

primarySymptoms: Profound, persistent fatigue and generalized weakness that is worse at the end of the day and does not improve with sleep — present in over 95% of patients with primary adrenal insufficiency.
Unintentional weight loss averaging 5-10 kg over months, from reduced appetite, nausea, and cortisol-dependent gluconeogenesis impairment.
Orthostatic hypotension and dizziness on standing, caused by aldosterone deficiency reducing blood volume in primary insufficiency, and cortisol deficiency reducing vascular tone in all forms.

Skin and mucosal hyperpigmentation — a pathognomonic finding of primary adrenal insufficiency; particularly prominent on sun-exposed areas, skin creases, buccal mucosa, and surgical scars, driven by excess ACTH stimulating melanocytes.
Salt craving — an unusual conscious preference for salty foods from aldosterone deficiency and sodium depletion.
Nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, and diarrhea — non-specific symptoms that worsen during intercurrent illness and may be the presenting signs of an evolving adrenal crisis.
Hypoglycemia — symptomatic low blood glucose, particularly in the fasting state or in children, from reduced cortisol-dependent counter-regulation of insulin.
Myalgia, arthralgia, and muscle cramps from electrolyte disturbance (hyponatremia, hyperkalemia) and reduced cortisol anti-inflammatory activity.
Depression, anxiety, and cognitive slowing — neuropsychiatric symptoms occur in 50-70% of patients and improve substantially with cortisol replacement.
Loss of axillary and pubic hair and reduced libido in women from loss of adrenal androgen (DHEA-S) production, which accounts for 50% of circulating androgens in women.
earlyWarningSigns: Progressive unexplained fatigue, anorexia, and weight loss over weeks to months in an otherwise healthy person — the insidious onset mimics depression or malignancy.

Hyponatremia (sodium below 135 mEq/L) with a mildly elevated potassium (above 5.0 mEq/L) on routine electrolyte panel without an obvious renal or GI cause — classic electrolyte pattern of primary insufficiency.
Skin hyperpigmentation in unusual sites (gum margins, tongue, surgical scars, knuckle creases) without sun or medication exposure — a near-pathognomonic early sign.
Low blood pressure (systolic below 100 mmHg) with a history of unexplained weight loss and salt craving.
Unexplained hypoglycemia in an adult without diabetes, especially during minor illness or fasting.
emergencySigns: Acute hypotension with systolic blood pressure below 90 mmHg that does not respond to IV fluids — adrenal crisis must be assumed and empirical hydrocortisone 100 mg IV given immediately without waiting for cortisol levels.

Altered consciousness, severe vomiting, or inability to take oral medications during acute illness in a known adrenal insufficiency patient — a sick-day rule emergency requiring immediate IV hydrocortisone.
Cardiovascular collapse with cold extremities, confusion, and bradycardia in a patient who has been recently stopping long-term steroid therapy — urgent cortisol replacement needed.

Severe hypoglycemia (blood glucose below 2.8 mmol/L) with loss of consciousness in a patient with known or suspected adrenal insufficiency — treat with IV glucose and IV hydrocortisone simultaneously.

causes.cause: Autoimmune adrenalitis (isolated or APS types 1 and 2)
causes.description: Autoimmune destruction of the adrenal cortex accounts for 70-90% of primary adrenal insufficiency in Western countries. Adrenal cortex antibodies (anti-21-hydroxylase antibodies) are detectable in 80-90% of cases. Addison's disease associates with other autoimmune conditions in autoimmune polyglandular syndrome type 2 (with autoimmune thyroid disease, type 1 diabetes) and type 1 (with hypoparathyroidism and chronic mucocutaneous candidiasis).

The most common cause globally in high-TB-prevalence settings; hematogenous spread of Mycobacterium tuberculosis destroys the adrenal cortex over years. Adrenal glands are enlarged with calcification on CT in early-active TB; atrophied and calcified in chronic burned-out disease. Anti-TB therapy halts progression but does not restore adrenal function once destroyed.

The most common cause of adrenal insufficiency worldwide, present in patients on prednisolone, dexamethasone, budesonide (inhaled or GI tract), and topical steroids at high doses. Suppression of the HPA axis begins within days of high doses and may persist for months to years after discontinuation. Tapering protocols reduce risk.

Pituitary macroadenomas and their treatment (transsphenoidal surgery, stereotactic radiotherapy) are the most common causes of secondary adrenal insufficiency. Radiation-induced pituitary dysfunction may evolve over 1-25 years after treatment. Lymphocytic hypophysitis from immune checkpoint inhibitor immunotherapy is a rapidly growing cause.

Bilateral adrenal hemorrhage in sepsis (Waterhouse-Friderichsen syndrome from Neisseria meningitidis), anticoagulant therapy, antiphospholipid syndrome, or adrenal vein thrombosis causes abrupt acute adrenal insufficiency. CT adrenal imaging showing bilateral hyperdense adrenal masses in the appropriate clinical context is diagnostic.

Cytomegalovirus adrenalitis, cryptococcal adrenal infiltration, mycobacterial disease (MAC), Kaposi sarcoma, and lymphoma can destroy the adrenal cortex in advanced HIV. Adrenal insufficiency affects up to 15-20% of patients with AIDS not on antiretroviral therapy.

riskFactors.factor: Other autoimmune disease (type 1 diabetes, autoimmune thyroid disease)
riskFactors.description: Presence of type 1 diabetes or autoimmune thyroid disease raises the risk of autoimmune adrenalitis by 5-10 fold through shared autoimmune polyglandular syndrome pathways. The combination of type 1 diabetes and hypothyroidism in a young woman should trigger adrenal screening.

riskFactors.factor: Chronic corticosteroid therapy (>5 mg prednisone/day for >4 weeks)
riskFactors.description: The dominant risk factor for tertiary insufficiency globally. Risk is directly proportional to dose and duration. Equivalent-dose inhaled budesonide above 800 mcg/day, high-potency topical corticosteroids, and intra-articular injections can all suppress the HPA axis with repeated use.

Active TB and even treated latent TB can destroy the adrenal glands. HIV-TB co-infection substantially elevates this risk. Regions with TB prevalence above 50 per 100,000 should have higher clinical suspicion for TB adrenalitis in adrenal insufficiency cases.

Pituitary tumors, craniopharyngioma, Rathke's cleft cyst, and all forms of pituitary surgery or radiotherapy cause secondary adrenal insufficiency in 50-80% of cases. All patients who undergo pituitary surgery require post-operative morning cortisol testing.

Anti-PD-1, anti-PD-L1, and anti-CTLA-4 agents (nivolumab, pembrolizumab, ipilimumab) cause immune-related adverse events including hypophysitis and primary adrenalitis in 2-8% of patients, producing secondary or primary adrenal insufficiency. Monitoring cortisol during immunotherapy is essential.

Advanced HIV enables opportunistic infections (CMV, cryptococcus, MAC) and neoplasms (Kaposi sarcoma) to infiltrate the adrenal glands. Effective antiretroviral therapy dramatically reduces this risk.
Autoimmune Addison's disease has a female-to-male ratio of approximately 3:1, consistent with the higher general autoimmune disease prevalence in women.

affectedDemographics: Women aged 30-50 are most commonly affected by primary autoimmune adrenal insufficiency, consistent with the pattern of other organ-specific autoimmune diseases.
Adults in high-TB-burden countries (sub-Saharan Africa, South Asia) are more likely to have TB-related primary adrenal insufficiency than autoimmune adrenalitis.
Patients with type 1 diabetes have a 1-2% lifetime risk of developing autoimmune Addison's disease (APS-2); this is several hundredfold higher than the general population.
Patients on immunotherapy for cancer: approximately 2-8% develop pituitary or adrenal immune-related adverse events with newer checkpoint inhibitor agents.
Patients on long-term systemic or high-dose inhaled corticosteroids: up to 1% of the population in developed countries takes doses sufficient to suppress the HPA axis.
diagnosisOverview: The Endocrine Society and APS guidelines recommend measuring an early morning (8-9 a.m.) serum cortisol as the initial screen. A cortisol above 500 nmol/L (18 µg/dL) effectively excludes adrenal insufficiency; below 100 nmol/L (3.6 µg/dL) in a sick patient is highly suggestive. The standard low-dose or high-dose ACTH stimulation test (Synacthen test or cosyntropin test) confirms the diagnosis: in primary and tertiary insufficiency, cortisol fails to rise above 500 nmol/L at 30 or 60 minutes after 250 µg IV or IM synthetic ACTH. The high-dose (250 µg) test misses mild secondary insufficiency; the low-dose (1 µg) test has superior sensitivity in these cases. ACTH measurement distinguishes primary (elevated ACTH above 200 pg/mL) from secondary and tertiary (low or inappropriately normal ACTH). Plasma aldosterone and renin differentiate primary (low aldosterone, high renin) from secondary (normal aldosterone, normal renin) forms. Anti-21-hydroxylase antibody, if positive, confirms autoimmune etiology. CT of the adrenal glands differentiates causes: bilateral adrenal enlargement with calcification in TB or fungal infiltration; atrophy in autoimmune or iatrogenic disease; hemorrhage appears as bilateral hyperdense adrenal masses. MRI pituitary with gadolinium is performed when secondary insufficiency is confirmed to identify tumors, Sheehan's syndrome, or hypophysitis. An incomplete morning cortisol recovery after glucocorticoid withdrawal requires a formal stimulation test before declaring the HPA axis recovered.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Initial screen; cortisol above 500 nmol/L excludes the diagnosis; below 100 nmol/L strongly supports it in a symptomatic patient
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw timed for 8-9 a.m. when cortisol is at its diurnal peak. Results within 1-3 hours. Concurrent illness, depression, and obesity can elevate cortisol and reduce sensitivity.

diagnosticTests.test: High-dose Synacthen/cosyntropin stimulation test (250 µg IV or IM)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Definitive confirmation of adrenal insufficiency; cortisol peak below 500 nmol/L at 30 or 60 minutes is diagnostic
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood drawn before, then at 30 and 60 minutes after an injection of synthetic ACTH. Results within 2-3 hours. Performed as an outpatient test; no fasting required.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Distinguishes primary (ACTH > 200 pg/mL) from secondary/tertiary (low or low-normal ACTH) adrenal insufficiency
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood collected in EDTA tube on ice; transported immediately to the lab to prevent ACTH degradation. Results within 24-48 hours in most centers.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies mineralocorticoid deficiency in primary insufficiency; elevated renin with low aldosterone confirms need for fludrocortisone
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood draw; patient should be supine for 30 minutes before collection. Certain drugs (ACE inhibitors, diuretics) affect results; review with the laboratory.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms autoimmune etiology; positive in 80-90% of autoimmune Addison's disease; absent in other causes
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw; results within 5-10 days. Positive result makes further workup for infectious or infiltrative causes unnecessary in most clinical contexts.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies bilateral adrenal enlargement (TB, fungal, hemorrhage) or atrophy (autoimmune, iatrogenic); differentiates metastatic disease
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 30-minute CT scan of the abdomen with IV contrast; adrenal protocol focuses thin cuts on the adrenal glands. Results within 24-48 hours.

treatmentOverview: Adrenocortical insufficiency requires life-long hormone replacement, with precise attention to dose and sick-day dose escalation. Glucocorticoid replacement is the cornerstone: hydrocortisone is the preferred agent because its short half-life best mimics the diurnal cortisol rhythm; the standard dose is 15-25 mg/day divided as a larger morning dose (approximately 10-15 mg upon waking) and a smaller afternoon dose (approximately 5-10 mg at 12-14:00), with no late-evening dose to avoid insomnia. Prednisolone 5-6 mg once daily as an alternative has the convenience of once-daily dosing but less physiological mimicry. Dexamethasone is generally avoided for long-term replacement due to high potency and poor diurnal mimicry. Endocrine Society 2016 guidelines recommend the lowest effective hydrocortisone dose (ideally 15-20 mg/day total) to avoid iatrogenic Cushing's features and bone loss. For primary adrenal insufficiency, mineralocorticoid replacement with fludrocortisone 50-200 µg once daily is essential; dose is titrated by blood pressure response, plasma renin, and sodium/potassium levels. DHEA replacement (25-50 mg/day) for women with primary insufficiency may improve mood, energy, and libido, though the Endocrine Society 2016 guideline gives this a conditional recommendation given limited evidence. Sick-day rules are the most critical patient education point: any fever above 38°C, vomiting, surgical procedure, or serious physical injury requires doubling or tripling the hydrocortisone dose (typically 20 mg every 6-8 hours for 24-48 hours during illness); if oral intake is not possible, patients must self-inject hydrocortisone 100 mg IM from an emergency kit. All patients should wear a medical alert bracelet and carry a steroid emergency card. Adrenal crisis is treated with immediate IV hydrocortisone 100 mg bolus followed by 200 mg/24h continuous infusion or 50 mg every 6 hours, plus IV saline with dextrose.

medicalTreatments.name: Hydrocortisone (15-25 mg/day in divided doses)
medicalTreatments.description: Physiological glucocorticoid replacement mimicking the diurnal cortisol rhythm. Given as a larger morning dose upon waking and a smaller afternoon dose; no late evening dose. Dose adjustments for illness, surgery, and physical stress are essential to prevent adrenal crisis.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Normalizes cortisol-dependent physiology in essentially all patients; quality-of-life measures improve significantly over pre-treatment; mortality risk from adrenal crisis approaches zero with adequate sick-day rules

medicalTreatments.name: Fludrocortisone (50-200 µg once daily) — primary insufficiency only
medicalTreatments.description: Synthetic mineralocorticoid replaces aldosterone in primary adrenal insufficiency. Dose titrated to normalize standing blood pressure, plasma renin, and serum sodium/potassium. Excess fludrocortisone causes hypertension, edema, and hypokalemia.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Corrects hyponatremia, hyperkalemia, and orthostatic hypotension within days; well-tolerated long-term at correct doses

medicalTreatments.name: DHEA (25-50 mg/day orally) for women with primary adrenal insufficiency
medicalTreatments.description: Adrenal androgen replacement in women who report persistent fatigue, reduced libido, and low mood despite optimal glucocorticoid and mineralocorticoid replacement. Not indicated in men (testicular DHEA production preserves normal androgen levels) and not universally endorsed; Endocrine Society 2016 recommends a 6-month trial with reassessment.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Randomized trials show modest improvement in mood and well-being in women; no consistent effect on bone density or fatigue

medicalTreatments.name: Emergency hydrocortisone 100 mg IM (self-injection kit)
medicalTreatments.description: A pre-loaded hydrocortisone injection carried by all patients with confirmed adrenal insufficiency for use when vomiting prevents oral dosing or during sudden collapse. Must be accompanied by emergency guidance and practiced injection technique.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Adrenal crisis mortality falls from >20% to near zero when emergency hydrocortisone is administered promptly in prehospital settings

medicalTreatments.name: Modified-release hydrocortisone (Plenadren, Chronocort)
medicalTreatments.description: Once-daily prolonged-release hydrocortisone formulations that better mimic the morning cortisol surge and provide low overnight levels. Trials show improved quality of life and better glycemic profiles vs. immediate-release formulations.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Phase 3 trials: statistically significant improvement in quality-of-life scores; HbA1c improvement in patients with coexistent diabetes

medicalTreatments.name: IV hydrocortisone 200 mg/24h infusion for adrenal crisis
medicalTreatments.description: Adrenal crisis treatment: 100 mg IV bolus immediately, followed by 200 mg/24 hours continuous infusion (or 50 mg every 6 hours by bolus). Combined with 1-2 L normal saline with 5% dextrose. Tapering begins when hemodynamically stable, usually at 24-48 hours.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reverses hemodynamic collapse within 2-4 hours in the vast majority of cases; mortality near zero with prompt treatment

alternativeTreatments.name: Licorice extract (glycyrrhizinic acid)
alternativeTreatments.description: Glycyrrhizin inhibits 11-beta-hydroxysteroid dehydrogenase type 2, extending cortisol half-life. Not evidence-based for adrenal insufficiency treatment and has significant blood pressure and electrolyte risks at pharmacological doses; not recommended.

whySeeSpecialist: All patients with confirmed or suspected adrenal insufficiency should see an endocrinologist to determine the cause, establish the correct hormone replacement regimen, and provide comprehensive sick-day education. Secondary causes require a full pituitary hormone panel and pituitary MRI. Annual review with an endocrinologist is recommended to adjust doses and screen for co-occurring autoimmune diseases.
doctorSelectionGuide: Choose an endocrinologist with specific experience in adrenal disorders; most academic medical centers have dedicated adrenal specialists. The Endocrine Society publishes a find-a-physician directory. For TB-related or HIV-related adrenal insufficiency, infectious disease co-management is important.
hospitalCriteria: Emergency department with IV hydrocortisone available immediately for adrenal crisis presentations
Endocrinology inpatient or consult service for complex diagnostic workup
ACTH stimulation testing capability within the institution
Adrenal CT and pituitary MRI radiology services
Long-term follow-up endocrinology clinic with experience in APS management
keyFacilities: Academic endocrinology centers with adrenal specialty expertise
Hospitals with 24-hour cortisol and ACTH assay availability
Pituitary programs for secondary insufficiency patients
Patient education programs for sick-day rule training and emergency kit dispensing
preventionStrategies: Screen for adrenal insufficiency in all patients with autoimmune polyglandular syndrome components (type 1 diabetes, autoimmune thyroid disease, vitiligo) annually with morning cortisol.
Taper corticosteroids gradually after prolonged use — abrupt discontinuation precipitates tertiary insufficiency; follow published tapering protocols and test the HPA axis before stopping.
Screen cortisol in all patients on immunotherapy (anti-PD-1, anti-CTLA-4) at baseline and with any suggestive symptoms to detect immune-related hypophysitis early.
Treat active tuberculosis promptly to prevent adrenal destruction; screen cortisol in all patients with active disseminated TB.
Vaccinate against pneumococcal disease and influenza to reduce the risk of intercurrent infections that trigger adrenal crises in known insufficiency.
lifestyleModifications: Take morning hydrocortisone immediately upon waking before getting out of bed, to prevent early morning hypotension and fatigue.
Memorize sick-day rules: double or triple hydrocortisone dose at onset of any febrile illness, injury, or surgical stress; use the emergency injection kit if oral intake is impossible.
Wear a medical alert bracelet stating 'Adrenal Insufficiency — needs immediate hydrocortisone' at all times; carry an emergency steroid card.
Inform all healthcare providers including dentists of your adrenal insufficiency diagnosis before any procedural or surgical intervention requiring additional cortisol coverage.
Avoid high-intensity unaccustomed exercise without extra hydrocortisone cover until well-established on replacement; discuss exercise stress dosing with your endocrinologist.
Maintain regular sleep-wake cycles to take advantage of the largest morning hydrocortisone dose timing with physiological diurnal needs.

recommended: Salt-liberalized diet with adequate sodium intake (above 3 g/day), particularly in hot weather or with exercise, to supplement fludrocortisone effect and maintain blood pressure
Regular carbohydrate intake distributed throughout the day to prevent cortisol-deficiency-related hypoglycemia between doses
Calcium-rich foods (dairy, fortified plant milks, leafy greens) and vitamin D (800-1,000 IU/day) to protect bone density in patients on long-term hydrocortisone
Adequate potassium intake (fruits, vegetables) in patients on higher-dose fludrocortisone to prevent hypokalemia
avoid: Skipping meals while on hydrocortisone, as missed cortisol dosing combined with fasting increases hypoglycemia risk
High alcohol intake, which can cause hypoglycemia and mask early adrenal crisis symptoms
Excessive licorice (true licorice root containing glycyrrhizin), which can alter mineralocorticoid activity and blood pressure in patients on fludrocortisone
Very high sodium restriction in patients on fludrocortisone — this may trigger excessive renin activation and worsens blood pressure control

exerciseGuidelines: Moderate regular exercise is safe and beneficial for long-term wellbeing in adrenal insufficiency. Patients should discuss extra hydrocortisone dosing (typically 5-10 mg extra pre-exercise) for planned prolonged intense exercise. The risk of exercise-induced adrenal crisis from unplanned vigorous activity is low with adequate replacement, but patients should recognize fatigue disproportionate to exertion as a warning sign to take extra hydrocortisone and rest.
dailyManagement: Take morning hydrocortisone dose immediately upon waking; take afternoon dose no later than 2-3 p.m. to avoid nighttime insomnia from delayed cortisol effect.
Check blood pressure standing and sitting daily at home when adjusting fludrocortisone dose; report persistent systolic below 100 mmHg standing.
Keep the emergency IM hydrocortisone kit accessible at all times — at home, at work, in a travel bag. Check expiry dates every 6 months.
Attend annual endocrinology review; bring a list of current doses, blood pressure readings, and any illness episodes.
Monitor for symptoms of over-replacement (weight gain, insomnia, bruising, new hypertension) and under-replacement (persistent fatigue, weight loss, dizziness).
Test blood glucose if hypoglycemic symptoms occur during illness — adrenal insufficiency-related hypoglycemia requires prompt glucose intake and often extra hydrocortisone.
prognosis: With appropriate hormone replacement and consistent sick-day education, most patients with adrenal insufficiency have an excellent quality of life and near-normal life expectancy. A large Swedish registry study (Björnsdottir et al., BMJ 2014) found that all-cause mortality in primary adrenal insufficiency is approximately 2-fold higher than the general population, primarily from adrenal crisis and cardiovascular disease, underscoring the importance of crisis prevention. Adrenal crisis accounts for roughly 6-8 events per 100 patient-years despite treatment; patient-held injection kits reduce crisis-related mortality to near zero. Quality of life on current replacement regimens remains below the general population average even when biochemically well-controlled, with fatigue and reduced vitality as persistent complaints. Modified-release hydrocortisone and DHEA replacement improve subjective well-being in clinical trials. Patients with TB-related adrenal insufficiency who are treated before complete cortical destruction may recover partial adrenal function; autoimmune destruction is complete and irreversible. Life-long follow-up is required.
recoveryTimeline: Symptoms of fatigue, weight loss, and hypotension typically improve within days to weeks of initiating correct hormone replacement. Full symptomatic recovery may take 3-6 months as nutritional status and blood pressure normalize. Skin hyperpigmentation in primary insufficiency fades gradually over 6-18 months of good replacement as ACTH levels fall. Adrenal crisis — when treated promptly — resolves hemodynamically within 4-8 hours of IV hydrocortisone.
complications: Adrenal crisis: the most serious complication — precipitated by any physiological stressor without adequate glucocorticoid cover; mortality 6-20% even in treated patients who fail to escalate doses; prevented by sick-day rules and emergency injection kits.
Osteoporosis from supraphysiological hydrocortisone dosing over years; DEXA scan every 2-5 years recommended; vitamin D and calcium supplementation and bisphosphonates in high-risk patients.

Cardiovascular disease: registry studies show approximately 1.8-fold higher cardiovascular mortality in adrenal insufficiency, from chronic under- or over-replacement altering glucose metabolism, vascular tone, and inflammation.

Autoimmune polyglandular syndrome: primary adrenal insufficiency is associated with autoimmune thyroid disease (Hashimoto's or Graves'), type 1 diabetes, premature ovarian insufficiency, pernicious anemia, and celiac disease — screen annually.
Hypoglycemia during illness in patients with coexistent diabetes or growth hormone deficiency who do not appropriately escalate glucocorticoid doses.
Iatrogenic Cushing's syndrome from chronic over-replacement with hydrocortisone, causing weight gain, striae, hypertension, and accelerated bone loss — requires dose optimization.

supportResources.name: NADF — National Adrenal Diseases Foundation
supportResources.description: US patient organization providing Addison's disease information, doctor referral, and emergency ID card programs.

supportResources.name: Addison's Disease Self Help Group (ADSHG, UK)
supportResources.description: UK patient charity with sick-day rules guides, patient forums, and healthcare provider education on adrenal crisis.

supportResources.name: Endocrine Society — Patient Resources: Adrenal Insufficiency
supportResources.description: Evidence-based patient information based on the 2016 Endocrine Society clinical practice guidelines.

confusedWithConditions.name: Adrenal crisis (Addisonian crisis)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Adrenal crisis is the acute life-threatening decompensation of adrenal insufficiency, not a separate diagnosis. It represents inadequate cortisol during physiological stress in a patient with known or undiagnosed insufficiency. The underlying chronic insufficiency is the primary diagnosis; crisis is the emergency presentation that requires immediate treatment without waiting for laboratory confirmation.

confusedWithConditions.name: Hypothyroidism
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Both cause profound fatigue, weight gain (hypothyroid) or loss (adrenal), and bradycardia. Hypothyroidism shows elevated TSH and low T4; adrenal insufficiency shows morning cortisol below 100 nmol/L and fails the Synacthen test. Hashimoto's thyroiditis and adrenal insufficiency co-occur in APS-2, so testing for both is appropriate when one is found.

confusedWithConditions.name: Sepsis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Sepsis and adrenal crisis can be nearly identical in presentation — hypotension, hypoglycemia, fever, confusion. The key difference is that adrenal crisis responds dramatically and specifically to hydrocortisone 100 mg IV, while sepsis requires source control and antibiotics as the primary therapy. In practice, empirical hydrocortisone is given in any unstable shock patient where adrenal insufficiency cannot be excluded.

confusedWithConditions.name: HIV/AIDS
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Advanced HIV can cause adrenal insufficiency through opportunistic infections, but HIV itself does not cause it directly. Unexplained fatigue, weight loss, and hypotension in an HIV-positive patient should trigger morning cortisol testing, as adrenal involvement in AIDS is under-recognized and treatable.

relatedConditions.name: Adrenal crisis
relatedConditions.relevance: The acute life-threatening complication of adrenal insufficiency; emergency management is distinct from chronic replacement.

relatedConditions.name: Hypothyroidism
relatedConditions.relevance: Co-occurs with adrenal insufficiency in autoimmune polyglandular syndrome type 2; both must be assessed and treated together.

relatedConditions.name: Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus
relatedConditions.relevance: Part of autoimmune polyglandular syndrome type 2; patients with type 1 diabetes have elevated risk of developing adrenal insufficiency.

relatedConditions.name: Hyperthyroidism
relatedConditions.relevance: Both adrenal insufficiency and hyperthyroidism cause weight loss, fatigue, and palpitations — distinguishing them requires thyroid and adrenal hormone testing.

relatedConditions.name: HIV/AIDS
relatedConditions.relevance: Advanced HIV causes adrenal insufficiency through opportunistic adrenal infiltration; recognition and treatment substantially improve outcomes.

faqs.question: What is adrenocortical insufficiency?
faqs.answer: Adrenocortical insufficiency is a condition where the adrenal glands produce too little cortisol. Primary forms (Addison's disease) also lack aldosterone. It can result from autoimmune destruction, tuberculosis, pituitary disease, or long-term steroid use. Without treatment it can cause life-threatening adrenal crisis.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of adrenal insufficiency?
faqs.answer: Common symptoms include severe fatigue, weight loss, loss of appetite, nausea, low blood pressure, dizziness, and salt craving. Primary adrenal insufficiency causes skin darkening, especially on skin creases, gum margins, and scars. Mood changes, muscle pain, and low blood sugar also occur.

faqs.question: What is the difference between primary and secondary adrenal insufficiency?
faqs.answer: Primary adrenal insufficiency (Addison's disease) is caused by damage to the adrenal glands themselves, causing both cortisol and aldosterone deficiency with elevated ACTH and skin darkening. Secondary insufficiency results from low ACTH due to pituitary or hypothalamic disease, preserving aldosterone with no skin darkening.

faqs.question: What causes adrenal insufficiency?
faqs.answer: The most common cause globally is autoimmune destruction of the adrenal glands. Tuberculosis is the leading cause in high-TB regions. Long-term corticosteroid therapy is the most common cause of secondary (tertiary) insufficiency worldwide. Pituitary tumors, surgery, and immune checkpoint inhibitor cancer therapy also cause it.

faqs.question: How is adrenal insufficiency diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis starts with an 8 a.m. serum cortisol. The ACTH stimulation test (Synacthen test) confirms insufficiency when cortisol fails to rise above 500 nmol/L at 30-60 minutes. Plasma ACTH level distinguishes primary from secondary forms. Anti-21-hydroxylase antibodies confirm autoimmune adrenalitis.

faqs.question: What is hydrocortisone replacement for adrenal insufficiency?
faqs.answer: Hydrocortisone is the standard treatment, taken orally 15-25 mg per day in divided doses. The largest dose is taken upon waking; a smaller dose at midday. It replaces the cortisol the adrenal glands cannot make. Doses must be increased during illness, injury, or surgery to prevent adrenal crisis.

faqs.question: What are sick-day rules for adrenal insufficiency?
faqs.answer: During fever, vomiting, injury, or surgery, the usual hydrocortisone dose must be doubled or tripled. If oral intake is impossible, an emergency hydrocortisone injection (100 mg IM) should be given immediately. Sick-day rules are the most critical safety education for every patient with adrenal insufficiency.

faqs.question: What is an adrenal crisis?
faqs.answer: Adrenal crisis is a life-threatening emergency in which cortisol levels are critically low, causing severe hypotension, vomiting, confusion, and potentially fatal cardiovascular collapse. It is triggered by physiological stress in a person with undiagnosed or inadequately managed adrenal insufficiency. Immediate IV hydrocortisone 100 mg is the treatment.

faqs.question: Does adrenal insufficiency cause skin darkening?
faqs.answer: Yes, but only in primary adrenal insufficiency (Addison's disease). When the adrenal glands fail, ACTH rises dramatically and stimulates melanocytes, causing hyperpigmentation of skin creases, gum margins, surgical scars, and sun-exposed areas. Secondary and tertiary forms do not cause skin darkening.

faqs.question: Do people with adrenal insufficiency need fludrocortisone?
faqs.answer: Only patients with primary adrenal insufficiency need fludrocortisone because only primary insufficiency causes aldosterone deficiency. Secondary and tertiary forms preserve aldosterone production via the renin-angiotensin system. Fludrocortisone 50-200 mcg daily restores sodium retention and blood pressure in primary insufficiency.

faqs.question: Is adrenal insufficiency life-threatening?
faqs.answer: Untreated or under-treated adrenal insufficiency can cause fatal adrenal crisis. Registry data show all-cause mortality is approximately 2-fold higher than the general population. However, with correct hormone replacement, sick-day education, and an emergency injection kit, patients can live a near-normal life.

faqs.question: Can long-term steroid use cause adrenal insufficiency?
faqs.answer: Yes. Chronic use of prednisone, dexamethasone, or high-dose inhaled corticosteroids suppresses the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis. When steroids are stopped abruptly, the adrenal glands cannot produce enough cortisol. The risk rises with doses above 5 mg prednisone daily for more than 4 weeks.

faqs.question: What medical alert should someone with adrenal insufficiency carry?
faqs.answer: All patients with adrenal insufficiency should wear a medical alert bracelet stating the diagnosis and the need for emergency hydrocortisone. They should also carry a steroid emergency card explaining the illness and treatment. These measures allow healthcare providers to act immediately if the patient is unconscious.

faqs.question: Can autoimmune adrenal insufficiency coexist with other autoimmune diseases?
faqs.answer: Yes. Autoimmune polyglandular syndrome type 2 (APS-2) includes primary adrenal insufficiency with type 1 diabetes and autoimmune thyroid disease. APS-1 includes adrenal insufficiency with hypoparathyroidism and mucocutaneous candidiasis. All patients with primary autoimmune adrenal insufficiency should be screened for these associated conditions annually.

faqs.question: How does tuberculosis cause adrenal insufficiency?
faqs.answer: Tuberculosis spreads hematogenously to the adrenal glands and destroys cortical cells over months to years, accounting for most adrenal insufficiency cases in high-TB-prevalence regions. CT shows bilateral adrenal enlargement with calcification in active TB adrenalitis. Anti-TB therapy prevents further destruction but cannot restore lost adrenal function.

simpleName: Adrenal insufficiency (adrenal gland failure, Addison's disease)
metaDescription: Adrenocortical insufficiency: cortisol deficiency from Addison's disease or steroids. Hydrocortisone replacement and crisis prevention reviewed by aihealz.
